food_subgroup: Rice dish with deep-fried marinated pork chop — steamed jasmine rice topped with garlic-soy-five spice battered deep-fried pork loin chop and pickled cabbage or soy-braised egg side

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for Taiwanese pork chop rice (pai gu fan). Closest parent concept: rice dish with fried pork.
ontology.foodex2_description: Composite dish based on cereals, rice and pasta, with meat, poultry and game
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Taiwanese pai gu fan. Closest parent: composite rice dish with meat. Code A0010X2 is for 'Composite dish based on cereals, rice and pasta, with meat, poultry and game'.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for this specific Taiwanese dish. Not assigned.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Taiwanese pork chop rice comprising approximately 200g steamed jasmine rice, 130g deep-fried marinated pork loin chop, and 50g pickled cabbage or soy-braised egg side, totalling approximately 380g and ~741 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — cooked white rice (SR Legacy #20444), pork loin chop deep-fried (SR Legacy #10062), pickled cabbage; composite dish calculation cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables for Taiwanese dishes
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or FAO/INFOODS entry exists for the complete Taiwanese pai gu fan composite dish. Values are derived by component-weighted calculation: 200g cooked jasmine rice (USDA SR Legacy #20444), 130g deep-fried pork loin chop (USDA SR Legacy pork loin, breaded and fried), and 50g pickled cabbage (USDA SR Legacy #11109 sauerkraut as closest analogue), with soy sauce marinade sodium contribution estimated from USDA SR Legacy soy sauce (#16124). Per-100g values represent the blended composite at 380g total serving weight. Cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA regional data for plausibility.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — white rice (GI ~73) adjusted downward for fat and protein co-ingestion from deep-fried pork chop batter; analogous composite rice-with-fried-meat dish estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Taiwanese pai gu fan in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at ~68 using white jasmine rice as the primary carbohydrate vehicle (GI ~73 per University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice, boiled). The presence of ~7.5g fat/100g and ~10g protein/100g from the deep-fried pork chop and batter moderately attenuates the glycemic response relative to plain rice, consistent with published fat-protein co-ingestion literature (Brand-Miller et al.). Glycemic load per 380g serving calculated as: (GI 68 × 87.4g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 59.4, rounded to 59.0. This GL is very high and clinically significant.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content — approximately 1,824mg sodium per 380g plate (480mg/100g), primarily from soy sauce marinade penetration into pork chop, pickled cabbage, and cooking salt in rice
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Taiwanese pork chop rice; if consumed, request low-sodium soy marinade, skip table dipping sauce, substitute steamed vegetables for pickled cabbage to reduce sodium by ~200mg, and limit portion to half-rice (100g) to reduce total meal sodium load toward a more manageable ~1,200mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load of 59 per plate driven by 87.4g available carbohydrate from 200g white jasmine rice (GI ~68); represents a substantial postprandial glucose challenge
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should request a half-rice portion (100g rice instead of 200g) to reduce glycemic load to approximately 30, pair with the protein-rich pork chop to further blunt glycemic response, and monitor postprandial blood glucose; full-portion pai gu fan is not recommended for routine consumption in individuals with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combined cardiovascular risk from high sodium (~1,824mg/plate), deep-frying oil contribution to total fat (7.5g fat/100g, 28.5g per plate), saturated fat (~7.6g per plate), and cholesterol (~114mg per plate); oil type (palm vs soybean) materially affects saturated fat and PUFA profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of Taiwanese pork chop rice due to its high sodium, elevated total fat from deep-frying, and saturated fat content; if consumed, choose restaurants using soybean or sunflower oil rather than palm oil, request reduced marinade, and pair with a non-pickled vegetable side to partially offset the sodium and fat burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying oil type determines inflammatory potential — palm oil (high palmitic acid SFA) is pro-inflammatory; soybean or sunflower oil provides more favourable PUFA profile
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should prefer restaurants using soybean or sunflower oil for deep-frying rather than palm oil, and limit frequency of consumption given the overall high fat load from deep-frying.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Taiwanese Pork Chop Rice (排骨飯, pái gǔ fàn) is one of Taiwan's most iconic and ubiquitous everyday rice dishes, served at self-serve canteens (自助餐 zì zhù cān), railway station bento boxes (鐵路便當 tiě lù biàn dāng), school cafeterias, and airline meal boxes across Taiwan. A standard plate (~380g) consists of approximately 200g steamed jasmine rice, 130g deep-fried marinated pork loin chop, and 50g pickled cabbage or a soy-braised egg side. The pork chop (排骨 pái gǔ) is a thin-sliced boneless pork loin chop marinated in garlic, soy sauce, and five spice powder, then coated in a light wheat flour batter and deep-fried to a crispy golden crust. The texture contrast between the crunchy battered exterior and the juicy, flavourful pork interior over soft steamed rice is the defining characteristic of this dish. Nutritionally, one plate provides approximately 741 kcal, 38g protein, 28.5g fat (predominantly from deep-frying oil), and 87.4g carbohydrate. Sodium is very high at approximately 1,824mg per plate (480mg/100g), driven by soy sauce marinade, pickled cabbage, and cooking salt. The glycemic index is estimated at ~68 (medium) with a glycemic load of 59 per plate — clinically very high, comparable to nasi kandar and lu rou fan. Thiamine (vitamin B1) is notably high at 0.45mg/100g, reflecting pork as the richest common dietary B1 source. The dish is NOT halal (contains pork) and is dairy-free. Clinical caution is warranted for hypertension (very high sodium), type 2 diabetes (very high GL), and cardiovascular disease (high sodium, fat, and saturated fat). Requesting half-rice (100g) reduces GL to approximately 30. Source: USDA FoodData Central component calculation cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA. Confidence: HIGH.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for the complete Taiwanese pai gu fan composite dish. Values are derived from component-weighted calculation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked jasmine rice, deep-fried breaded pork loin, and pickled cabbage, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA regional data for plausibility. GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database with fat-protein co-ingestion adjustment. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact composite dish entry was found in any single approved primary source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite deep-fried pork chop rice dish is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA as of 2026; the split between MUFA and PUFA depends heavily on the specific frying oil used by each restaurant (palm oil vs soybean oil vs sunflower oil), making a single defensible value not possible without laboratory analysis. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in available approved sources. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible/zero per USDA SR Legacy data for the component ingredients under standard commercial frying conditions). All core macros (protein_g 10.0g, fat_g 7.5g, carbohydrate_g 23.0g) are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for this composite rice-and-fried-pork dish. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate — no direct GI measurement for pai gu fan exists in the University of Sydney GI Database; estimation basis documented in gi_source. Sodium of 480mg/100g equates to approximately 1,824mg per 380g plate — clinically very high. This dish is NOT halal (contains pork). The alias 'Taiwanese braised pork rice' in the input is a misnomer (that term more accurately describes lu rou fan / 滷肉飯); it has been retained as an alias for retrieval purposes but is flagged as a misnomer. Input description field incorrectly described this as 'slow-cooked pork belly' — corrected to deep-fried pork loin chop per dish definition.
